sentences.text: Whenever.
Am I OK?
No.
Yes.
There are some people that don't know how they were saved.

Most people do know the way they were saved because if they had a paper in their hand, they knew that either it came from Wallenberg, either it came-- they-- many times they didn't even know that it was a duplicate.
That they didn't know, because we have made duplicates.
I have worked nights in printing shop on-- I remember the street-- [INAUDIBLE] or [INAUDIBLE] Street 49, downstairs.
It was a Jewish [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] gymnasium was like a college type.
It was-- and the basement, we had access to it.
And of course, we had secret passages getting in there.
And we copied, but they didn't know.
However, the last thing we would have wanted to have is any names, any lists, because if one of us is caught-- and there were many of us that were caught, shot on the spot, thrown into the Danube.
The list would have committed them to death.
So why keep a list?
It's the last thing we wanted to know.
No.
But how did you keep order?
How did you somehow focus on the people who needed to be helped?
Well, my-- everyone had different assignment.
I was not the only-- there were several hundred, 200, 300 people that assisted the Wallenberg organization.
And everyone had different assignment.
Everyone did things.
My specific assignment was limited to two things.
I helped in the printing of the fake passport and then I helped in the delivery of the fake passport.
The reason I was able to deliver them, because I could get away easier as a Gentile because of my knowledge of the Hungarian language.
As I mentioned to you, at home, we spoke Hungarian, so I spoke as fluently Hungarian as a native Budapest kid who's still not in the Army but is a Gentile kid.
So I always had Gentile papers with me if I was stopped, OK?
Unless if I was searched, that was a death sentence.
But if I was not searched and I showed them the Gentile papers, most of the time I got away with it.
And then I was small.
I was a small kid.
And I was-- I knew the city.
I figured out the sewer system.
When we had some engineers, some people that knew more than even I did, and they guided me, which sewer system, how I'll get into that building.
And I was more or less the delivery, the messenger who delivered these papers into these protected houses.
We were able to get them in a lot of times without papers.
But then to get them out, there were always guards at the door.
It was much harder to get them out.
When you brought them in, they always thought, oh, let them be there.
They're-- they're behind the gate.
We'll get them.
But to get them out, they had to have something in their hands.
So that was our job, or this is my job to get it to them so they can get out and then hand it over to a different group.
And each group didn't always know what the other group is doing.
We just had specific assignment without knowing too much.
If we knew too much, under torture we may have given away too much information and I may have condemned somebody.
So I didn't want to-- need to know more than what my job was.
Beyond fear.
It was beyond fear.
Yes, fear was constant.
But when you are-- when the fear is constant and you are ready to-- you know that you're going to get killed if you're caught, something strange happens.
You live with that fear.
How can I compare it to-- it's-- it's uncomparable to anything else.
Yes, I knew I would get killed instantly if I'm caught.
But this was a mission.
I was a kid, but this was my mission to-- I-- I volunteered for this.
Some people didn't.
Some people were selfish and hide out in the woods.
Some people changed into Gentile papers and-- and befriended a Gentile family who took them in.
Some people joined churches.
Everybody did whatever they could to survive.
This was my choice.
And this was my choice.
I lived with that fear, but not to the point where I become dysfunctional.
I functioned with fear, constantly with fear.
But I did not let fear stop me from doing what I believed in doing.
I have to tell you some interesting little stories.
But there are thousands of them and we would take days to describe all of the daily happenings.
But, for example, we always had problems where to sleep, because by this time, I did not go back to the place where my mother found me this job, and I had no place to sleep at.
We slept in different places.
But if we slept in a place where we were exposed though, we were just machine-gunned.
We were shot down immediately, because death was not by trial.
That was, you're guilty, walk you to the Danube, tie one hand to another guy, because they wanted to save the bullet.
Shoot one guy in the back and two of them fall in in the river.
The river was flooded with bodies.
So that was-- execution was immediate.
So it was-- the biggest problem was, where do we sleep?
We discovered-- there were six of us.
We discovered that the universities, somehow the Germans respected and they allowed the university to function as an autonomy.
They did not enter university grounds.
And we became acquainted with a caretaker.
Of course, we paid off.
We always had some access to some rich Jews that have assisted us secretly.
And we paid off the caretaker of the morgue.
And that's where we slept for about six months.
So we got in through the background, [INAUDIBLE].
Once we were inside the university, we were not afraid.
But the safest place even in the university was in the morgue.
And it was many nights that I slept on those same slabs where dead ones were lined up.
And I just slept, you know, one of those slabs.
They didn't have modern drawer type of slabs.
All the slabs were exposed.
It was 60 years ago, 50 years, 55 years ago.
But that was the safest place where we could find.
And for several months that's where we slept.
And they brought in the dead ones on the [INAUDIBLE].
I mean, they didn't bring Jews there.
They threw them in the river.
But from the university, for research and so forth.
It didn't mean anything.
I mean, we were exposed to death continually.
So just an interesting story how you always find a way to resolve a problem.
These were intimate friends.
There were many others that I didn't know well.
But these were six-- six of them that we--
One is in Israel that I know of.
The rest, I don't.
Ernest Hollander.
I'm sorry.
He's now in this country.
He came from Israel to this country.
But Ernie Hollander.
Ernie Hollander is-- you may have heard or-- it was all over the news where this man who appeared on, oh, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] about a year, year and a half-- a year and a half ago.
And he was supposed to face the skinheads, fighting with them because they claim that there was no Holocaust and so forth.
And he appeared on that show.
I did not want to do it.
I refused to go, because I don't believe to give them the time of-- my time.
But nevertheless, he did.
And so he was on all stations over and over again.
And eventually-- I'm only saying it because you may have heard it, that eventually the was shown in New York where someone from Yugoslavia--
Now you know the story.
OK.
Yugoslavia has seen this Ernie and he says, you know, he's talking about his brother.
He looks alike and his brother was telling us in Yugoslavia that he has a brother that died.
But he-- this must be the brothers, but they don't know about each other.
And through the Arsenio Hall, he discovered 50 years later that he has a brother that he always considered to be dead.
Well, that story was--
And that is the guy that I'm talking about.
That is Ernie Hollander, who's my friend.
He was my friend and we worked together in Budapest.
Yes.
Yeah, because--
I wish there were more happy endings.
I knew you would know about that story, because it was very well documented on-- on TV.
Yes.
Yes.
Yes.
He came around occasionally into the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the homes that he had.
But he only came for encouragement.
He only came around for a very short time.
And I didn't want him to know who we are, because we didn't want him-- we didn't want to expose him to the fact that we-- there was an organization that duplicated.
Why throw that burden on him?
He knew it.
But you just don't speak about it.
So yes, I have met him, at least three or-- two or three different occasions, briefly.
But never have I discussed with him or told him what I am doing or what we are doing.
While he knew all along there couldn't be that many passports, because he knew how many he issued and so many more people had it.
So it was the kind of thing that if we tell him, what about if they turn around and they torture him, and-- yeah.
We didn't-- it was best not to speak of it.
I met him, spoke to him.
He was very kind man, compassionate person, very calm, cool, very, very cool.
And young man, very handsome young man.
Well, he was sent-- as I understand, King Gustav has given him diplomatic degree or diplomatic position.
And he came with just a couple knapsacks and a rolled-up blanket, because he didn't know what he was going to find in Budapest.
Well, but he speak with such authority that he stood up against Eichmann and Eichmann respected that.
But then, I have later on learned after the war there were other reasons, too, that he made it very clear that the Wallenberg family [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] is-- are the bankers in this country.
And the Germans have not put the valuables that they have confiscated from the Jews in their own banks, because they were constantly being destroyed, bombarded, so forth.
So they put it in the Wallenberg family vaults.
And he told Eichmann, you destroy me and all those vaults will blow up.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And if Hitler finds out that you caused the destruction of so many artifacts which belonged to him and Himmler and to other things, you will be killed too.
Now, this story I've heard later on, that secretly that's how he gained the power.
But we didn't know that at that time.
But one thing we did know, that it was unbelievable that a German would respect someone like they respected him.
They-- There were others, too, that did it.
Red Cross did it.
There were International Red Cross had.
And even there was Danish, Swedish.
There were about four or five diplomats that tried to do.
But no one, no one came near what Wallenberg did.
They were copying his system.
But they were not dedicated and the Germans did not respect them.
Occasionally, there was Red Cross, an International Red Cross flag and the International Red Cross gave them some documentation.
They came and emptied out the building without question.
They never did it to one of his buildings.
No.
I've read so much about the documentation, about the books, and about people that were saved by him.
I think as-- everything has been already documented and-- and there's very little that is-- does not exist.
All you have to do is just search for books that have been written.
There have been about six, seven of them that have been written about him, about his activities, about his life in Budapest.
And today, it's-- everything is known.
There's very little that I have intimate information or new information that I could add to it, because remember, I-- I was not working for him.
I was working within the larger scope of an organization.
And that is what got me deported also, eventually.
We'll get to it and I'll explain you about--
OK.
Well, what has happened, I've done this for several months, for seven, six, seven months.
I-- I can't remember the exact timing.
But on one occasion-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was several times I had very, very close calls.
We're just surrounded-- usually, in Hungarian the name was called [HUNGARIAN].
It was where the Germans would surround four corners of a street, OK?
And then they would surround four outside corners.
And anybody within that, they would gather up and they would have to prove that they are not Jews.
And if they were caught, they were condemned.
Well, I have been many times in one of those situations.
But I escaped them over and over and over and over again, and was very fortunate I escaped them every time.
This one time, it was early November in 1944.
I was delivering a packet full of papers to-- to one of the buildings, which was close to the Danube.
Most of them were alongside the Danube, because Jewish people owned those buildings which were [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] alongside the Danube, very close there.
Nevertheless, I made an error.
Tired, exhausted.
And instead of coming up with my-- in the fox hole in middle of this courtyard, I came out in front of the courtyard.
And, of course, I tried to get back.
But by that time, one of the Hungarian soldiers grabbed me by the neck, pulled me up, and immediately started-- pulled me towards the gate and started searching my pockets.
He finds about six, seven false documents and another one with my picture on it that I'm a Gentile.

And what was worse, he finds a letter that a son wrote to his mother that he begged me that I should deliver to his mother who-- who was in that courtyard that-- and I didn't bother reading the letter.
I accepted that I would do that.
And when they opened up the letter, they started reading where this son tells him that he is aware that the Russians are outside and the Hungarians and Germans will be killed in no time.
The Hungarian-- the Russians, just listen, Mom, to the bombardment of the Russians and just a question of days, so hold out.
We will be liberated.
I am OK and you will be liberated.
And it gave her encouragement, but blasted away at Hungarians.
Well, immediately they knew who I am and what I'm doing, because they knew of us, that we exist.
But occasionally when they caught someone, they shot him.
So as they're reading, they started laughing.
And they were taking sort of bets.
Well, whose pigeon is it going to be, because you shoot pigeons.
You shoot people like pigeons.
Oh, you shot the last one.
This is mine.
This guy is mine.
And so-- and-- and I, understanding fluently what they were talking, knew that it's a question of minutes.
They would handcuff me, take me to the Danube, probably tie me to somebody else, and shoot me.
I faced that.
But I was also trained to take certain actions [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] under that station like that, which were unpredictable.
So what I did at that time, they placed their guns-- they didn't have pistols.
They had long, long old-fashioned guns.
And they placed them against the wall as they were reading and checking all these documents.
And I just had seconds to think what I'm doing.
What I did, I kicked the guns as hard as I could.
They flew up in the air.
And I started running.
Get down on my knees, jump up, run across the street, confusing them.
Well, it took them maybe 10 seconds to recover the guns, 15 seconds takes-- you know, 'til they pulled the guns and they start aiming.
Literally heard bullets flying by me.
But now I ran into a busy street.
And they were shooting, but they were reluctant to shoot at other people while they were aiming at me.
And I realized suddenly that there was what's called a transport.
Transport meant a group of Jews that are being deported somewheres, either into a camp or-- or being deported to the train.
I had no idea where they were being taken.
But there were Hungarian soldiers on both sides.
And there were like 50 to 75 Jews on the streets that were being walked somewheres.
So quickly on my hands and knees got up and started marching with them.
And I literally marched by these two soldiers that were running around like mad looking for me to get me.
But one thing, they never looked into this transport because, I mean, he's not in there, he's running away from us, while I'm marching by this building.
Just as I passed the building, within a few minutes, two trucks pull up.
Back opens up and about 40 SS soldiers jump out.
And they surround the whole group.
My thought was, I escaped so many times death, that I escape one more time.
Let me do this for the moment.
This is for now.
Next step, I'll do something else, because I didn't even know where I'm going.
And-- but once the Germans surrounded the transport, I knew I'm in trouble.
I cannot escape.
There were 40, 50 of them.
They jumped off the back of the truck.
They surrounded.
And we continued marching.
So I continued matching looking for a way how can I get out.
It was-- it was an impossibility.
It was a sure death if I tried to move out of the line.
And within four, five minutes, six minutes marching, I find myself that we are on the railroads, in the railroad tracks.
And the trains are waiting.
And we are being pushed into the train.
Actually, these Jews were being sent for deportation to Auschwitz, not into another ghetto.
Well, I had no opportunity to get out.
I was just shoved in, into the cattle train with the rest of them.
Jokingly, I always say, I deported myself.
So in November 1944, I deported myself.
But there was no alternative.
And that was the end of my career in Budapest.
And now I was going to Germany.
Well, I didn't know really where they were taking us.
But they took us to Buchenwald, not Poland.
Because remember, Poland was half-liberated.
The Russians were on outskirts.
Auschwitz was already liberated by that time.
But I didn't know that.
Yes.
Yes.
Yes.
And this was way-- this was November '44.
January, February '45.
January '45 Poland was-- yeah.
Auschwitz was liberated '45.
No, it was not liberated at that time, yet, no.
But they did not bring that many Jews any more, because the war was on in Poland.
And they were pushed-- they were outside of-- of Budapest, the Russian soldiers.
The Russians have already bombarded day and night Budapest to take the city.
They took it in January.
They took Budapest in January.
That's when Budapest fell.
However, they took us to Buchenwald.
The reason to Buchenwald is because they needed more labor, the Germans.
They killed-- they were so ambitious killing so many that they ran out of people to do certain labor that they needed.
Of course, I knew nothing of this.
I only knew that I'm being deported to Buchenwald.
And the train ride was probably the most horrible experience of all experiences that I've had.
I'm sure you have heard it from others.
Each person has their story, and ours was horrible.
It was four, possibly five days.
I cannot remember it.
Totally without any food.
We must have been about 150 in-- in this cattle car.
And what was the most horrible thing is the stench of the people that died, the screaming of the children.
There were women that had their babies.
Hunger.
They were weak.
There were older people.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Heart attack, anything.
Name it.
And we could not unload.
Once in 24 hours or 36 hours, whenever they felt to, they allowed us to unload it then.
But most of time, they didn't, because they wanted to bring them into Buchenwald and then cremate the dead, so we had to ride out with the dead people.
Probably one of the most horrible experiences were when there were older men that immediately took over, tried to make some-- some semblance of order on the train.
But even so, I was only at that time 16 years old.
I knew what's happening.
Women were having their babies.
And they had to kill their own children.
They did kill, because they knew they cannot survive.
They had no milk.
They had no food.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Knowing, hearing-- and the only thing that I knew that we heard children screaming and there was silence.
So one of the guys would say, well, this child is dead.
Then we found out that it was either the elders or sometimes the mothers themselves just killed their own children, because they knew more than we did.
See, I was a kid.
I was in-- in underground.
I was not as wise as they were.
They knew more.
They knew that they were going to be probably annihilated and most were.
The weaker ones were immediately killed.
So why bring the child also to be killed or to be burned?
Then several of us tried to escape through that little window that is no more than like 24 inches long and maybe 10, 14 inches wide, just enough for a body to squeeze through.
There were three of us.
And we decided, well, we're going to jump.
Well, the first one that jumped just-- everything was all right before the jump.
Just as we pushed him through the window, we looked down and it was a huge ravine.
He must have fallen 1,000, 1,500 feet.
We knew he was instant death.
Well, the second guy says, I will still give it a try.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the second guy, we assisted.
I was the third one behind him.
He got on my shoulder, stuck his head through that little window.
No more than 10 seconds goes by and a bullet blows his head off.
So I just-- I saw his head scatter.
So pushed-- with somebody's help, pushed his body out.
There was no point in-- there was no saving him.
I got discouraged and I didn't jump, but I was supposed to be the third one.
And I was brought into Buchenwald about five, six days later.
No, we had no idea.
No, we had no idea.
We had no idea where it's going.
Absolutely no idea.
No, my family went to Auschwitz.
My family was deported to Auschwitz.
My mother, father--
When the group was being rounded up in Budapest I thought--

No, the group was being rounded up and I didn't know whether they're being taken to a ghetto, because what happens, they formed ghettos and some groups they took into ghettos to accumulate them up for a larger transport.
Some were being transported immediately.
No one knew where they're taking or who they're taking where.
I had no idea where this group is being taken when I jumped in to save myself.
And when I was on the train, the train just kept riding.
We saw that we are not going east, because some people, older people recognized that we are going west.
We suspected we were going into Germany.
But we had no idea where into Germany we were going or why we are going.
That's the last thing that-- that we ever knew where we are going.
They were not-- they took a lot of people from the outlying areas.
Somehow, our transport was taken to Buchenwald.
I never knew the reason why.
The only thing that I could think of, that they needed labor, because we immediately were put to work.
And I will explain you what happened.
After debarkation in-- in-- in [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Buchenwald, after we unloaded all the dead ones, there were maybe 20% that didn't make it, possibly more.
What they did, they brought us into camp.
And it was at night.
I'll never forget.
It was a cold, winter night.
It was November.
It was very cold in Buchenwald.
And the first thing they did, they undressed us They took every possession what we had.
We didn't have anything, papers, a picture, a keys, or something.
But they took everything away from us.
And for hours they let us stand in the nude.
There is nothing as demoralizing and dehumanizing as standing cold in the nude, men and women together, and not knowing what next, when the door will open up, what next will be.
Finally, after several hours, people were falling.
They were freezing.
They were screaming.
[INAUDIBLE] They took us to a room where they had a huge tub, a gigantic tub, which had a solution in it.
Probably, I believe, it was DDT dissolvant.
And there were two Germans standing on the side.
And one by one we had to go through that tub, dunk into the tub.
Kept us down for about a second or two.
And it was burning.
It was horrible.
And then wet, get out again, and wait.
Many, many people just drowned.
They just wanted to drown.
They didn't want to come out.
That was-- that was de-licing or whatever they believed in.
And then several hours passed by more and we looked into-- we were able to look into one of the barracks.
And we saw thousands of them huddling on the floor.

And we were just praying to God that if you give us no more than just opportunity to get in-- into this barrack to be able to sit on the floor where all these people are huddling and warming each other, that that would be saving us for now.
But it took a long, long time before that happened.
